1: 'Come near, O nations, and hear! Listen, O peoples! Let the earth hear and everything in it — the world and all that springs from it' — the universal summons to witness divine judgment: 'qirveu goyim lishmo'a, draw near, O nations, to hear.' The court-summons formula (Psalms 50:1: 'the Mighty One, God the LORD, speaks and summons the earth from the rising of the sun to where it sets'). All four are summoned to hear: goyim (nations), ammim (peoples), erets (earth), and melo'ah (its fullness = everything on earth, cf. Psalms 24:1: 'the earth is the LORD's and everything in it'). The comprehensive summons parallels the beginning of major covenant lawsuits (Deuteronomy 32:1: 'Listen, you heavens, and I will speak; hear, you earth, the words of my mouth'). Isaiah 34-35 forms a bookend pair: Isaiah 34 = judgment on the nations/Edom; Isaiah 35 = restoration of Israel.
2: 'For the LORD is enraged against all the nations and furious against all their armies; he has devoted them to destruction and given them over to slaughter' — the universal scope of divine wrath: 'ki qetsaf YHWH al-kol-hagoyim, for YHWH is enraged against all the nations.' The qetsef (fury/rage = the intense anger that burns like fire) 'against all the nations' (kol-hagoyim) = the comprehensive judgment of the Isaiah Apocalypse (Isaiah 13-23 judged the nations individually; here the entire category is condemned). 'He has devoted them to destruction' (vehechariym otam, he has devoted them to the ban/herem) — the herem (sacred destruction = the complete dedication of defeated enemies to YHWH through total annihilation, the same vocabulary as Joshua 6:17-21's Jericho and Deuteronomy 7:2's Canaanite-conquest commands). The war-ban applied to all nations = cosmic holy war.
3: 'Their slain will be cast out, and the stench of their corpses will rise; the mountains will be soaked with their blood' — the aftermath of cosmic judgment: 'vechalleleyhem yushlaku, their slain will be cast out.' The casting out of the unburied dead = the ultimate dishonor in the ANE; denial of burial was a severe curse (Deuteronomy 28:26: 'your carcasses will be food for all the birds and the wild animals'). 'The stench of their corpses will rise' (mippigereiyhem ya'aleh ba'asham, from their corpses their stench will go up). 'The mountains will be soaked with their blood' (venimassu harim middamam, the mountains will melt with their blood) — rivers of blood soaking the mountains = the scale of cosmic judgment exceeding any historical battle. This hyperbolic intensity signals the eschatological scope: not a single battle but the final settling of all accounts.
4: 'All the host of heaven will dissolve; the heavens will be rolled up like a scroll. All their host will fall down as a leaf falls from a vine, or as a fig drops from a fig tree' — the dissolution of the cosmic order: 'venimqahu kol-tseva hashamayim, and all the host of heaven will dissolve/rot.' The tseva hashamayim (host of heaven = the sun, moon, and stars — and perhaps the angelic powers behind the nations, cf. Isaiah 24:21). 'Venikpelu kassefer hashamayim, and the heavens will be rolled up like a scroll' — the scroll-metaphor for heaven: the sky rolled up like a papyrus or parchment scroll being closed. Revelation 6:14 quotes this directly: 'The heavens receded like a scroll being rolled up.' The falling host: 'as a leaf falls from a vine' (kenibol aleh miggefen) and 'as a fig drops from a fig tree' (ukhinbol mitte'enah = the autumn fig that falls with the slightest touch when fully ripe). The immense stars/powers fall as easily as dead leaves.
5: 'For my sword has been bathed in heaven; see — it descends on Edom, on the people I have devoted to destruction, for judgment' — the sword of divine judgment specifically targeting Edom: 'ki rivetah beshamayim charbi, for my sword has been saturated/watered in the heavens.' The divine sword 'watered' (riva, from ravah = to drink one's fill, to be saturated with blood) in heaven before its earthly descent — the judgment prepared in heaven before it lands on earth. 'See — it descends on Edom' (hineh al-edom terade, behold it will descend on Edom). Edom (the descendants of Esau, dwelling south and east of the Dead Sea) is the paradigmatic enemy of Israel: Gen 25-36 (Jacob and Esau's enmity); Psalms 137:7 ('Remember, LORD, what the Edomites did on the day Jerusalem fell'); Obadiah (the entire book against Edom). 'The people I have devoted to destruction, for judgment' (ve'al-am chirmi lemishpat, and on the people of my ban/herem for judgment).
6: 'The sword of the LORD is filled with blood; it is gorged with fat — the blood of lambs and goats, the fat of rams' kidneys. For the LORD has a sacrifice in Bozrah, a great slaughter in the land of Edom' — the sacrifice metaphor for divine judgment: 'cherev laYHWH male'ah dam, the sword of YHWH is full of blood.' The military judgment described as a sacrificial meal: 'filled with fat' (huddeshenah mechelev, saturated with fat) — the blood and fat are the parts of the sacrifice that belong to YHWH (Leviticus 3:16-17: 'all the fat is the LORD's'; Isaiah 17:6). The animals listed are sacrificial animals: lambs (kevasim), goats (attudim), rams (ayilim) — the precise vocabulary of the peace-offering. 'The LORD has a sacrifice in Bozrah' (ki zevach laYHWH beBosra, for YHWH has a sacrifice in Bozrah) — Bozrah (modern Buseirah in Jordan, the Edomite capital city) is the slaughter-site. Revelation 19:17-18 and Ezekiel 39:17-20 develop the 'great supper/sacrifice' motif of divine judgment.
7: 'Wild oxen will fall with them, young bulls together with mighty bulls; their land will be soaked with blood, and their soil made rich with fat' — the extension of the sacrifice-imagery to wild animals: 'veyerdu remmim imam, and wild oxen will come down with them.' The re'em (the wild ox/aurochs — the most powerful wild bovine of the ANE, Job 39:9-12; Numbers 23:22; Deuteronomy 33:17 — 'the strength of a wild ox') alongside the domesticated bulls (parim ve'abbirim, young bulls and great bulls). The full spectrum of bovine power (wild to domestic, young to mature) all sacrificed. 'Their land will be soaked with blood' (vesirvetah artsam middamam, their land will be drunk with blood) and 'their soil made rich with fat' (vafaras adamatam mechalev, their soil will be saturated/made fat with fat) — the soil itself enriched by the divine sacrifice, as field soil is fertilized by organic matter.
8: 'For the LORD has a day of vengeance, a year of recompense for the cause of Zion' — the theological grounding of Edom's judgment: 'ki yom naqam laYHWH, for it is a day of vengeance for YHWH.' The naqam (vengeance = the righteous vindication of wrong done to a covenant party, the same naqam as 'vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord' in Deuteronomy 32:35; Romans 12:19; Hebrews 10:30). 'A year of recompense for the cause of Zion' (shnat shillumim leryv Tsiyon, a year of recompense for the dispute/legal cause of Zion). The legal terminology: Zion's legal case (riv = lawsuit/dispute) against Edom is being settled by divine recompense (shillumim = retribution/payment). Edom's cruelty at Jerusalem's fall (Psalms 137:7; Obad 11-14) is the specific cause being repaid. Isaiah 61:2 quotes the same 'day of vengeance of our God' in the Spirit-anointed servant's proclamation.
9: 'The streams of Edom will be turned to tar, its soil to sulfur, and its land will become burning pitch' — Sodom applied to Edom: 'vehafkhu nechaleiha lezefet, and its streams will be turned to tar/pitch.' The three substances: zefet (tar/pitch = the bituminous material of the Dead Sea region, Genesis 14:10: the Valley of Siddim full of tar pits), gofrit (sulfur = the burning sulfite of Genesis 19:24: 'YHWH rained down burning sulfur on Sodom and Gomorrah'), and yit-bahm (burning pitch = the self-sustaining fire). Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19) were destroyed by fire and sulfur from heaven — the most comprehensive divine judgment in the OT. By using the same vocabulary for Edom, Isaiah applies the Sodom-judgment to Edom. Edom's territory (south of the Dead Sea) was geographically adjacent to Sodom's site, reinforcing the connection.
10: 'It will not be quenched night or day; its smoke will rise forever. From generation to generation it will lie desolate; no one will pass through it, forever and ever' — the permanent, unquenchable destruction: 'laylah veyyomam lo tichbeh, night and day it will not be quenched.' The self-sustaining fire of divine judgment — no natural cause can extinguish it. 'Its smoke will rise forever' (leolamim ya'aleh ashanah, her smoke will go up forever and ever) — eternal burning smoke. Revelation 14:11 quotes this for the torment of those who worship the beast: 'the smoke of their torment goes up for ever and ever.' Revelation 19:3 quotes it for the fall of Babylon: 'the smoke from her goes up for ever and ever.' The perpetual desolation: 'from generation to generation it will lie desolate' (ledor vador tishammah, from generation to generation it will be desolate) = the total absence of any resettlement or recovery, forever.
11: 'The pelican and the porcupine will take possession of it; the owl and the raven will nest there. The LORD will stretch over it the measuring line of chaos and the plumb line of emptiness' — the wild-animal inventory of the desolate land: 'vireshuhah qa'at veqippod, the pelican and porcupine will possess it' (qa'at = the desert pelican or owl; qippod = the porcupine or short-eared owl). 'The owl and raven will nest there' (yanshuf ve'orev yishku vah, the owl and raven will dwell in her). But the theological climax: 'venathah aleyhah qav tohu ve'avnei vohu, he will stretch over her the measuring line of chaos and the plumb line of emptiness.' The qav tohu (measuring line of chaos) and avnei vohu (plumb line of emptiness) — the builder's measuring-line and plumb-stone (used to ensure accurate construction) here applied to anti-construction. The exact vocabulary of Genesis 1:2 (tohu vavohu, chaos and void) applied to Edom's end: creation is being unmade by the same instruments by which it was made. YHWH the builder unmeasures Edom back into pre-creation chaos.
12: 'They will call it, No Kingdom There, and all its princes will come to nothing' — the complete erasure of Edom's political structures: 'chorehav velo-sham malkut yiqre'u, its nobles — and no kingdom will be called there.' The chorehyah (its nobles/free-born = the aristocratic class, from chur = the white linen of the upper class) are stripped of status: 'no kingdom there' = the political entity of Edom simply ceases to exist as a recognizable kingdom. 'And all its princes will come to nothing' (vekol-sareyhah yihyu ayen, and all its princes will become nothingness). The sar (prince/official) reduced to ayin (nothing = the absolute non-being, the word used for non-existence). The double erasure: the kingdom-title gone, the individual officials reduced to nothing.
13: 'Thorns will overgrow its citadels, nettles and brambles its fortresses; it will become a haunt for jackals, a dwelling place for ostriches' — the vegetation-and-wildlife takeover: 've'altah armenoteiha sirim, thorns will overgrow its citadels' (armenoteiha = its citadels/palaces). The sirim (thorns) and qimmosh (nettles/weeds) and chadah (brambles/thistles) — the judgment-vegetation (Isaiah 5:6; Isaiah 7:23-25; Isaiah 9:18; Isaiah 10:17; Isaiah 27:4; Isaiah 32:13) overtaking the noble buildings. 'A haunt for jackals' (naveh tannim, a place of jackals/dragons) — the tannim (jackals/sea-monsters, used for both the desert-dwelling jackal and mythological dragons) in ruins is the standard desolation-image (Jeremiah 9:11; Isaiah 10:22; Isaiah 49:33; Isaiah 51:37). 'A dwelling place for ostriches' (chatsir livnot ya'anah, a courtyard for daughters of the owl/ostrich) — the wilderness bird that makes its nest in desolate places (Lamentations 4:3).
14: 'Wildcats and hyenas will encounter each other there; the wild goat will call out to his fellow. The night owl will also settle there and find her resting place' — the continuation of the fauna inventory: 'upaggeshu tsiyim et-iyim, wildcats will encounter hyenas there.' The tsiyyim (desert creatures/wildcats = the denizens of desolate wastes, Psalms 72:9; Isaiah 13:21) and iyyim (hyenas or howling creatures). 'The wild goat will call to his fellow' (vesair el-re'ehu yiqra, and the goat/satyr will call to his fellow) — the se'ir (the hairy/shaggy goat, but also the term for a goat-demon or satyr of ANE mythology, Leviticus 17:7: Israel must no longer offer sacrifices to the goat-demons). The demonic-goat's calling echoes the ANE understanding of ruins as the dwelling of demonic powers. 'The night owl' (lilit, the Lilith = the night-creature, related to layil = night; in later Jewish tradition Lilith became a female demon, the queen of the night) 'will settle there and find her resting place.'
15: 'There the great owl will make her nest, lay eggs, hatch, and gather her young under her shadow; and the hawks will assemble there, each with its mate' — the breeding cycle of the wilderness fauna completing the desolation: 'sham qinnena qippoz vattefel vatevqa, there the great owl will make her nest and lay eggs and hatch.' The detailed nesting-and-breeding sequence (nest → eggs → hatching → gathering young) = the permanence of the wild animal occupation. Not a temporary passing-through but a multi-generational settlement: the birds are raising families in the ruins. 'The hawks will assemble there, each with its mate' (sham niqqebbetsu dayot ishah re'utah, the kites/hawks will assemble, each with her companion) — the hawks gathering in pairs = the beginning of new families in the ruins. The animal breeding cycles demonstrate that the desolation will persist for generations.
16: 'Search and read the book of the LORD: not one of these will be absent, not one will lack its mate. For my mouth has issued the command, and his Spirit has gathered them' — the prophetic guarantee of fulfillment: 'dirshu missefer YHWH veqiru, search and read from the book of YHWH.' The 'book of the LORD' (sefer YHWH) may be the scroll of prophetic oracles (Isaiah 8:16-17; Isaiah 30:8) or the divine book of decrees. The instruction to verify: none of the animals listed will be absent, none without their mate. 'Ki-pi hu tsivah verucho hikkibbetsam, for his mouth commanded it and his Spirit gathered them' — the two agents of fulfillment: divine speech (pi hu tsivah, his mouth commanded it = the creative word of Gen 1: 'God said...and it was so') and divine Spirit (rucho, his Spirit = the Spirit who hovered over creation in Genesis 1:2 now gathering the animals to inhabit the de-created Edom). The Spirit who created gathers the animals to inhabit the un-creation.
17: 'He has cast the lot for them, and his hand has divided it for them by the measuring line; they will possess it forever; from generation to generation they will dwell there' — the divine land-distribution to the animals: 'vehu hippil lahem goral, and he cast the lot for them.' The lot-casting (goral = the sacred lot used to divide the land among Israel's tribes in Joshua 14:2; Isaiah 18:6) — the same mechanism Israel used to divide Canaan is used to distribute Edom's land to wild animals. 'His hand has divided it for them by the measuring line' (umiddo chalekah lahem baqav, and by measuring line he divided it for them) — the same measuring line of Isaiah 34:11 (the qav tohu) = the instrument of judgment also serves as the land-distribution tool. 'They will possess it forever; from generation to generation they will dwell there' (le'olam yiresheet, they will possess forever) — the animals receive the same 'forever' land-promise that Israel received for Canaan (Genesis 17:8; Leviticus 25:34).
